plainLanguageDescription: You get extra time to submit assignments without any penalty.
whatChangesInPractice: When an assignment is due, you have extra days to submit. Your grade is not affected. You do not need to explain yourself each time — the accommodation covers you.

commonObjections.objection: Other students do not receive extra time on assignments.
commonObjections.response: This adjustment levels the playing field. The extra time makes up for a real barrier — not a lack of effort.

commonObjections.objection: The deadline is the same for everyone to ensure fairness.
commonObjections.response: Equal treatment is not the same as fair treatment. Giving the same deadline to students with different barriers is not fair.

legalBasis.description: Institutions receiving federal funding must provide reasonable adjustments for documented disabilities that affect academic performance.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You get extra time during exams — usually 1.5x or 2x the standard — so tests measure what you know, not how fast you write.
whatChangesInPractice: Instead of the standard exam time, you are given extra time. This is arranged through disability services before each exam.

commonObjections.objection: Extended time gives an unfair advantage.
commonObjections.response: Extra time removes an unfair disadvantage. For students who process more slowly, standard timed exams measure speed rather than knowledge.

commonObjections.objection: We cannot accommodate extended time in our exam room.
commonObjections.response: Many institutions have a separate testing centre for this. I would like to discuss what options are available here.

legalBasis.description: Extended time is one of the most widely supported accommodations in higher education for students with processing, reading, or attention differences.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You take exams in a quieter, smaller space so the environment does not get in the way of showing what you know.
whatChangesInPractice: Instead of a large exam hall, you are placed in a smaller room with fewer distractions. This is arranged through disability services before each exam period.

commonObjections.objection: We do not have a separate room available.
commonObjections.response: I would like to work together to find a practical solution. Even a smaller classroom during the exam period would help.

legalBasis.description: Providing an appropriate testing environment is a standard adjustment for students whose conditions are affected by sensory or attention factors.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You receive important instructions in writing so you can refer back to them as many times as you need.
whatChangesInPractice: Verbal instructions given in class are followed up in writing — by email or on the course portal. This means you are never stuck trying to remember something you heard once.

commonObjections.objection: Instructions are already on the syllabus.
commonObjections.response: This covers verbal instructions given during class that are not always written down — things said in the moment that change or clarify the syllabus.

legalBasis.description: Providing written versions of verbal instructions is a low-cost, high-impact adjustment supported under reasonable accommodation requirements.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You can record lectures for your own use so you can replay and process the material at your own pace.
whatChangesInPractice: You may use a personal device or app to record audio or video of lectures. Recordings are for your personal use only.

commonObjections.objection: I do not allow recordings to protect my intellectual property.
commonObjections.response: The recording is strictly for this student's personal academic use and is not shared. This is a well-established accommodation that does not affect intellectual property in practice.

legalBasis.description: Permission to record lectures for personal use is a recognised adjustment for students with auditory processing, attention, or memory differences.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: A classmate shares their notes with you so that taking notes does not stop you from concentrating on the lecture.
whatChangesInPractice: Disability services arranges for a peer volunteer in your class to share notes. You still attend class — the notes support your own engagement rather than replacing it.

commonObjections.objection: Students should take their own notes as part of the learning process.
commonObjections.response: For students with working memory or auditory processing differences, taking notes can block them from processing the lecture. Peer notes allow full engagement with the material.

legalBasis.description: Peer note-taking is a standard support recognised under disability law for students whose conditions make simultaneous listening and writing difficult.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You can choose where you sit — near the front, near the door, or away from distractions — without needing to explain yourself.
whatChangesInPractice: Your accommodation letter notifies professors that you can choose your seat based on your needs. No justification required.

commonObjections.objection: Seating is assigned in this class.
commonObjections.response: Preferential seating is a standard accommodation that takes priority over assigned seating policies for students with documented needs.

legalBasis.description: Preferential seating is a widely recognised, low-impact adjustment for students with sensory, attention, or physical needs.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: Course materials are provided in a format that works for you — such as digital text, audio, or high-contrast documents.
whatChangesInPractice: Textbooks, readings, and handouts are made available in your preferred format. This may include digital PDFs, text-to-speech compatible files, or enlarged print.

commonObjections.objection: We only have print versions of these materials.
commonObjections.response: The institution has an obligation to provide accessible formats. Disability services can help source or convert materials.

legalBasis.description: Providing accessible format materials is a core requirement under disability law, particularly for students with print disabilities.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II, Chafee Amendment

plainLanguageDescription: You can use text-to-speech software to have written content read aloud to you.
whatChangesInPractice: You may use approved text-to-speech tools during class, assignments, and exams where reading is required.

commonObjections.objection: Using text-to-speech during exams gives an unfair advantage.
commonObjections.response: Text-to-speech removes a barrier for students with reading differences. It does not provide extra information — it makes existing text accessible.

legalBasis.description: Assistive technology is a recognised reasonable accommodation under disability law.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You can use speech-to-text software to dictate your written work instead of typing or handwriting.
whatChangesInPractice: You may use approved speech-to-text tools for assignments and exams. This lets you show your knowledge through spoken language.

commonObjections.objection: Written assignments require typing or handwriting.
commonObjections.response: Speech-to-text is an assistive technology accommodation. It does not change what is being assessed — it removes a barrier to showing that knowledge.

legalBasis.description: Speech-to-text is a recognised assistive technology accommodation for students with written expression or motor difficulties.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You have an agreed number of extra absences that do not count against your grade, for days when your condition makes attending genuinely impossible.
whatChangesInPractice: Your professor is told about your flexible attendance. A specific number of extra absences are permitted without penalty. This is a defined, agreed adjustment — not unlimited absences.

commonObjections.objection: Attendance is mandatory in this course.
commonObjections.response: Flexible attendance is a reasonable adjustment for students whose condition directly affects their ability to attend. I am not asking to skip class — I am asking for protection on days when attending is genuinely impossible despite my best efforts.

legalBasis.description: Flexible attendance is a recognised adjustment where attendance relates directly to a student's disability, provided it does not fundamentally change the course requirements.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You receive early reminders about upcoming deadlines so that difficulty tracking time does not cause you to miss work you are fully capable of completing.
whatChangesInPractice: Your professor or disability services sends you a reminder a set number of days before major deadlines. Simple to arrange, and it makes a real difference.

commonObjections.objection: Deadlines are already posted on the syllabus.
commonObjections.response: For students with time perception difficulties, knowing a deadline exists and being able to feel it approaching are different things. A proactive reminder bridges that gap.

legalBasis.description: Proactive deadline reminders are a low-cost reasonable adjustment for students with executive function or time perception differences.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: Large assignments are broken into smaller pieces with check-in points so the full size of a project does not become a barrier to starting.
whatChangesInPractice: For major assignments, you and your professor agree on smaller milestones. You submit portions at agreed intervals rather than everything at once. This does not reduce the work — it restructures how it is submitted.

commonObjections.objection: I cannot monitor interim submissions for every student.
commonObjections.response: This applies only to my work. The check-ins can be lightweight — even a brief email confirmation. The structure itself is what makes the difference.

legalBasis.description: Restructuring assignment submission as an accommodation for executive function difficulties is supported under reasonable adjustment requirements.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: You can show participation in ways other than speaking aloud in class — such as written responses, online discussion, or one-to-one check-ins.
whatChangesInPractice: Participation grades are assessed through agreed alternatives. You might post responses online, submit written reflections, or meet briefly with the professor instead of contributing verbally.

commonObjections.objection: Verbal participation is a learning outcome of this course.
commonObjections.response: I would like to discuss which parts of verbal participation are core outcomes and which are just one way to show engagement. There may be ways to meet the underlying goal through a different method.

legalBasis.description: Alternative participation methods are a recognised accommodation where standard requirements create a barrier related to a student's disability.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

formalName: Advance Notice of Schedule or Format Changes
plainLanguageDescription: You receive advance warning when class schedules, formats, or locations are going to change, so unexpected transitions do not derail your day.
whatChangesInPractice: Your professor notifies you in advance — ideally 48 hours or more — when there will be changes to the usual class format, location, or schedule. Even a same-day message makes a real difference.

commonObjections.objection: Sometimes changes are unavoidable and cannot be communicated in advance.
commonObjections.response: I understand not every change can be anticipated. The accommodation asks for advance notice when possible, and prompt communication when not. Even a short message helps.

legalBasis.description: Advance notice of changes is a reasonable accommodation for students whose disability involves difficulty with unexpected transitions.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, ADA Title II

plainLanguageDescription: If you do not have a formal diagnosis, you may still be able to access support through a self-disclosure process while working toward documentation.
whatChangesInPractice: You meet with a disability services coordinator to describe your difficulties in your own words. Provisional support may be put in place while you work on getting formal documentation. The coordinator can also connect you with low-cost or free evaluation resources.

commonObjections.objection: We require formal documentation before providing any accommodations.
commonObjections.response: I understand documentation is usually required. I would like to discuss whether provisional support is available while I work on getting it, and whether the office can help connect me with evaluation resources.

legalBasis.description: While documentation is standard, institutions have flexibility in how they gather information about a student's disability. Self-disclosure can be considered when documentation is unavailable.
legalBasis.reference: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, AHEAD Best Practice Guidelines
